Index Review Graph Issues
ACK timing before long-running agent work Actions on the wrong target machine or host can cause unsafe operational changes. Agents can mislead users if failures are reported as successful outcomes. Case-sensitive database comparisons can miss logically equivalent values. Changes can introduce regressions when not tested after modification Missing replay data compromises the ability to debug or verify past execution behaviors OpenClaw dispatch routing failures OpenClaw Slack replay and post-deploy stability failures Post-deploy stability smoke test run 'openclaw' resulted in an overall failure OpenClaw dispatch routing failures Stale caches can hide current behavior or serve outdated assets

Case-sensitive database comparisons can miss logically equivalent values.

When to Use

Use this guidance when designing database schemas, writing SQL queries for lookups, or enforcing uniqueness constraints where case differences should be ignored (e.g., usernames, email addresses, or tags).

Context

By default, many database systems perform case-sensitive string comparisons. This behavior can lead to logical errors where `Value` and `value` are treated as distinct entities. If the application logic treats them as the same, this discrepancy can cause data duplication, failed lookups, or integrity violations.

Procedure

To ensure comparisons and uniqueness checks ignore case:

1. Identify the columns requiring case-insensitive logic (e.g., `username`, `email`). 2. Apply a case-insensitive collation to the column definition or index.

  • In SQLite, use the `NOCASE` collation.
  • In MySQL, use an appropriate case-insensitive collation such as a `_ci` collation for the target character set.
  • In PostgreSQL, use a suitable case-insensitive strategy such as `citext`, a nondeterministic ICU collation, or a functional index on `lower(column)`, depending on schema and version constraints.

3. When performing ad-hoc queries, explicitly cast or use collation functions if the column defaults to case-sensitive.

Verify

Test lookups and constraints using mixed-case values:

  • Attempt to insert `User` and `user`; ensure the second insert fails if uniqueness is required.
  • Query for `USER` and ensure the record for `user` is returned.

Reusable Lessons

  • When database lookups or uniqueness checks should ignore case, use case-insensitive collation such as NOCASE for the relevant comparison or index.
  • Explicitly define collation at the schema level to avoid reliance on application-level normalization.

Agent Use

Use this page when:

  • Case-sensitive database comparisons can miss logically equivalent values.

Apply it by:

  • Use case-insensitive collation such as NOCASE for the relevant comparison or index.

Verify by:

  • Test lookups with mixed-case values.

Do not use it when:

  • The current evidence does not match the symptoms, scope, or provenance on this page.
